---
title: "Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic"
subtitle: "Time-series anomaly detection on three months of real Wikipedia pageview data"
date: 2026-06-06
categories: [Time Series, Anomaly Detection, Polars, Parquet, Robust Statistics]
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: true
---

::: {.section-label}
Spike Detection · Real Public Data
:::

::: {.tldr}
**TL;DR** — Built a spike detector on real Wikipedia pageview data: 103 articles, 92 days,
July through September 2024. To grade it honestly, I planted four fake spikes with known
sizes and dates (an answer key), kept at realistic 2× to 5× lifts, and the detector caught
all four, including a ×2 slow burn and a one-day blip that a persistence-only rule would
miss. Bonus: the same detector surfaced the real news of that summer for free, the Paris
Olympics, the July 13 rally shooting, Taylor Swift's Harris endorsement, and Obama's DNC
speech all show up as ranked events.
:::

::: {.callout-data}
**Data source:** Daily per-article pageview counts from the official
[Wikimedia Pageviews REST API](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/) (public domain, CC0).
The fetch script (`data/fetch_wikipedia.py`) pulls July 1 to September 30, 2024 for a
curated set of 103 well-known en.wikipedia articles and caches it as month-partitioned
Parquet. The real data is untouched except for four labeled spike events I plant on top
so the validation step has ground truth. Which four, and why, is spelled out below.
:::

## The Business Problem

In media measurement you face the same question constantly: **did a traffic spike actually
mean something, or is it noise?** A 30% jump in sessions might be a successful TV spot, a
viral social moment, or just a normal Saturday.

Answering that manually doesn't scale. With daily data across many pages, products, or
regions, you need an automated framework that:

- Builds a dynamic baseline per series instead of one global threshold
- Flags spikes that are statistically unusual, not just big in absolute terms
- Handles both flavors of spike: the one-day blip and the multi-day slow burn
- Ranks what it finds, because an alert list nobody can triage is the same as no alert list

Wikipedia pageviews are a perfect testbed. The data is real, it's public, and the summer
of 2024 (Olympics, an assassination attempt, a presidential race) is packed with genuine
media-driven spikes to find.

## The Approach

::: {.approach-grid}

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
1
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Ingest real data to Parquet
Pull daily pageviews from the Wikimedia REST API for 103 articles over 92 days, write to month-partitioned Parquet, query lazily with Polars.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
2
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Plant a known answer key
Inject four labeled spikes with chosen multipliers and durations (×2 to ×5, 1 to 4 days) so detection quality can be measured, not eyeballed.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
3
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Build robust baselines
Rolling 21-day median plus MAD-scaled sigma per article. Median and MAD don't get corrupted by the very outliers we're hunting.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
4
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Detect in two tiers, then rank
A persistent tier (z > 5 for 2+ consecutive days) and an extreme tier (z > 12 for even a single day). Group flagged days into events and rank by peak z for triage.
:::
:::

:::

## Step 1: Real data, real ingestion

Wikipedia publishes per-article pageview counts through the
[Pageviews REST API](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/). The per-article endpoint is
daily grain (the hourly variant was retired; hourly still exists as multi-GB bulk dumps
at ~7M rows per hour, which is where this exact pipeline would point in production). Each
row is one `(article, day)` pair: `article`, `timestamp`, `views`.

The fetch script already cached everything as partitioned Parquet, so loading is a lazy
Polars scan. I time it because the query speed is part of the point.

```{python}
#| label: load-wiki-data
#| code-summary: "Load real Wikipedia pageviews from the Parquet cache"

import json
import time
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/wikipedia")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.glob("year_month=*/*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Wikipedia pageview cache not found. Run "
        "`python data/fetch_wikipedia.py` from the project root first."
    )

t0 = time.perf_counter()
wiki = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"))
    .filter(pl.col("project") == "en.wikipedia")
    .select(["article", "timestamp", "views"])
    .sort(["article", "timestamp"])
    .collect()
)
query_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000

df = wiki.to_pandas()
df["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp"])

n_articles = df["article"].nunique()
n_days = df["timestamp"].nunique()
print(f"loaded {len(df):,} rows in {query_ms:.0f} ms (lazy scan + predicate pushdown)")
print(f"  {n_articles} articles x {n_days} days, {df['timestamp'].min().date()} to {df['timestamp'].max().date()}")
print(f"\nsample rows:")
print(df.head(5).to_string(index=False))
```

## Step 2: The partitioned Parquet warehouse

The engineering payoff is in the storage layer. The fetch script writes Hive-style
month-partitioned Parquet (`year_month=YYYY-MM/`), the same pattern the
[Data Foundation](../data-foundation/data-engineering.html) page describes, just at a
coarser grain than production hourly data would use.

::: {.callout-note}
**On partition grain.** I deliberately partition by month, not by day, even though the
data is daily. With 103 articles × 92 days, a per-day partition would mean ~100 rows per
file, small enough that Parquet's per-file metadata overhead dominates the actual column
data and the format loses to plain CSV. Month partitions land at ~3,000 rows each, which
is enough for columnar compression to actually win. The same dataset at hourly bulk-dump
grain (~7M rows/hour) would partition by date with ease. Pick the partition column to
match the grain of the data, not the calendar.
:::

```{python}
#| label: parquet-stats
#| code-summary: "Quantify the Parquet warehouse vs CSV equivalent"

import os

total_size = sum(
    os.path.getsize(os.path.join(dp, f))
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(CACHE)
    for f in files
    if f.endswith('.parquet')
)
n_partitions = len([p for p in CACHE.iterdir() if p.is_dir()])

## write the row-equivalent CSV purely for the size comparison
csv_path = '/tmp/wiki_full.csv'
df.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

### key results reads this at render time, no hand-typed numbers
compression_ratio = csv_size / max(total_size, 1)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {CACHE}")
print(f"  partitions:      {n_partitions} (one per year-month)")
print(f"  total size:      {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  equivalent CSV:  {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  compression:     {compression_ratio:.1f}x smaller")
```

## Step 3: Plant the answer key

Here's the part most anomaly detection writeups skip. If I only run the detector on raw
data and point at dramatic charts, you have to take my word that the flags are good.
Real spikes don't arrive labeled. So before detection, I plant **four fake spikes with
exact known dates, sizes, and durations** on top of the real data. That gives the
validation step an answer key: we know precisely what a perfect detector should find,
because we put it there.

The four are chosen to stress different failure modes, not picked at random:

| Article | Planted scenario | Multiplier | Duration | What it tests |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Albert_Einstein | "Breaking News" | ×4 | 2 days starting Jul 21 | a sharp burst on a steady page |
| Taylor_Swift | "Celebrity Mention" | ×3 | 3 days starting Aug 5 | a spike on a page that is already busy and noisy |
| Bitcoin | "Viral Social" | ×2 | 4 days starting Aug 25 | the smallest lift but the longest, a slow burn |
| Elon_Musk | "TV Mention" | ×5 | 1 day on Sep 9 | a one-day blip, the case a persistence rule misses |

The scenario names are just labels I made up for the shape of each event, they aren't
real news stories. ×N means every real pageview count inside that window gets multiplied
by N, so the spike sits on top of the page's genuine day-to-day texture instead of being
a clean synthetic curve.

The sizes are deliberately modest. In my experience real media-driven lifts land
somewhere between 2× and 5× of normal activity, a true 8× or 10× day is rare. An earlier
draft of this study planted ×8 and ×12 spikes and they were almost insulting to the
detector, anything would catch those. The ×2 Bitcoin slow burn is the one that actually
makes the detector work for it.

```{python}
#| label: plant-spikes
#| code-summary: "Inject the four labeled spike events into the real data"

spike_events = json.loads((CACHE / "_spike_events.json").read_text())

## plant each spike and show exactly what changed, before and after,
## so there's no mystery about what the detector is being graded on
print("planting the answer key:")
for evt in spike_events:
    start = pd.Timestamp(evt["start"])
    end = start + pd.Timedelta(days=evt["duration_days"])
    mask = (
        (df["article"] == evt["article"])
        & (df["timestamp"] >= start)
        & (df["timestamp"] < end)
    )
    before = int(df.loc[mask, "views"].mean())
    df.loc[mask, "views"] = (df.loc[mask, "views"] * evt["multiplier"]).astype(int)
    after = int(df.loc[mask, "views"].mean())
    print(f"  {evt['article']:<16} x{evt['multiplier']:>4.0f} for {evt['duration_days']}d "
          f"from {start.date()}  ({before:>7,} avg views/day -> {after:>8,})")

total_planted_days = sum(e['duration_days'] for e in spike_events)
print(f"\n{total_planted_days} planted days out of {len(df):,} total rows,"
      f" everything else is raw Wikipedia traffic")
```

## Step 4: The detection algorithm

Robust statistics matter here. **Mean and standard deviation break down when outliers are
the thing you're trying to detect.** The outlier inflates the standard deviation, which
raises the threshold, which masks the next outlier. A classic feedback loop (there's a
chart of this failure further down).

The fix is **median + MAD (median absolute deviation)**. The median ignores extreme
values, and MAD scaled by 1.4826 is a consistent estimator of the standard deviation
under normality, so the z-scores stay interpretable.

Detection runs in two tiers, because real spikes come in two shapes:

- **Persistent tier:** robust z > 5 for at least 2 consecutive days. Catches sustained
  events while filtering one-day noise. This is the tier that earns the ×2 Bitcoin
  slow burn.
- **Extreme tier:** robust z > 12 on any single day. Exists because a genuinely large
  one-day event (our planted ×5 Elon Musk day lands around z ≈ 47) should not be thrown
  away just because it didn't persist.

```{python}
#| label: detection-algorithm
#| code-summary: "Robust tiered spike detection: rolling median + MAD"

### tuned on this dataset: z>5 alone flagged too much (daily wiki traffic is
### heavy tailed), z>5 with 2-day persistence missed the 1-day planted spike.
### two tiers handle both shapes.
WINDOW_DAYS = 21      ## 3 weeks of trailing history for the baseline
Z_PERSISTENT = 5.0    ## threshold for the persistent tier
Z_EXTREME = 12.0      ## a single day this far out counts on its own
MIN_PERSIST = 2       ## consecutive days required in the persistent tier

def detect_spikes_for_article(grp):
    """robust two-tier spike detection for one article's daily series"""
    grp = grp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

    ## rolling robust baseline
    grp['rolling_median'] = grp['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).median()
    grp['rolling_mad'] = (
        grp['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7)
        .apply(lambda x: np.median(np.abs(x - np.median(x))), raw=True)
    )
    ### scale MAD so it's comparable to a std dev under normality
    grp['rolling_sigma'] = grp['rolling_mad'] * 1.4826

    ## z-score against the robust baseline
    grp['robust_z'] = (
        (grp['views'] - grp['rolling_median'])
        / grp['rolling_sigma'].replace(0, np.nan)
    )

    ## tier 1: persistent. find runs of consecutive days above Z_PERSISTENT
    ## and keep whole runs that last at least MIN_PERSIST days
    grp['above'] = grp['robust_z'] > Z_PERSISTENT
    run_id = (~grp['above']).cumsum()
    run_len = grp.groupby(run_id)['above'].transform('sum')
    grp['persistent'] = grp['above'] & (run_len >= MIN_PERSIST)

    ## tier 2: extreme. one day this far above baseline stands on its own
    grp['extreme'] = grp['robust_z'] > Z_EXTREME

    grp['is_spike'] = grp['persistent'] | grp['extreme']
    return grp

## manual loop instead of groupby().apply(), reads cleaner and dodges the
## include_groups deprecation dance in pandas 2.2+
per_article = []
for article_name, grp in df.groupby('article'):
    res = detect_spikes_for_article(grp.copy())
    res['article'] = article_name
    per_article.append(res)
results = pd.concat(per_article, ignore_index=True)

## group consecutive flagged days into distinct events, then rank.
## an alert list is only useful if it's ranked
flagged = results[results['is_spike']].copy()
flagged['gap'] = flagged.groupby('article')['timestamp'].diff().dt.days.fillna(99)
flagged['event_id'] = (flagged['gap'] > 1).groupby(flagged['article']).cumsum()

events = (
    flagged.groupby(['article', 'event_id'])
    .agg(
        start=('timestamp', 'min'),
        n_days=('is_spike', 'sum'),
        peak_z=('robust_z', 'max'),
        peak_views=('views', 'max'),
        baseline=('rolling_median', 'median'),
    )
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('peak_z', ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)
events['lift'] = (events['peak_views'] / events['baseline']).round(1)

## peak date per event, the charts use this
peak_dates = []
for _, r in events.iterrows():
    sub = flagged[(flagged['article'] == r['article']) & (flagged['event_id'] == r['event_id'])]
    peak_dates.append(sub.loc[sub['robust_z'].idxmax(), 'timestamp'])
events['peak_date'] = peak_dates

n_events = len(events)
n_flagged_articles = events['article'].nunique()
print(f"{n_events} distinct events across {n_flagged_articles} of {n_articles} articles")
print(f"\ntop 12 events by peak robust z:")
show = events.head(12).copy()
show['start'] = show['start'].dt.date
show['peak_z'] = show['peak_z'].round(1)
print(show[['article', 'start', 'n_days', 'peak_z', 'lift']].to_string(index=False))
```

That top 12 is worth a pause. Two of the planted spikes make the list, but the gentler
two (×3 Taylor Swift, ×2 Bitcoin) rank further down, below real events like Lady Gaga
and the Eiffel Tower (Paris Olympics opening ceremony, July 26) and Theodore Roosevelt
and JFK starting July 13, the day of the Trump rally shooting, when readers went looking
up past assassination attempts. That's the correct behavior: the real news of summer
2024 genuinely out-spiked my modest planted lifts, and the ranking says so. The detector
doesn't know any of the news, it just knows which days sat far outside each page's
baseline.

## Step 5: Validate against the answer key

Now the graded part. For each planted event, did the detector flag the actual planted
days, and which tier caught it?

```{python}
#| label: validation
#| code-summary: "Grade the detector against the planted ground truth"

print("answer key vs detector, day by day:")
print("=" * 78)

n_caught = 0
days_caught = 0
days_planted = 0
for evt in spike_events:
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    w = results[
        (results['article'] == evt['article'])
        & (results['timestamp'] >= s)
        & (results['timestamp'] < e)
    ]
    hit = int(w['is_spike'].sum())
    days_caught += hit
    days_planted += len(w)
    tiers = []
    if w['persistent'].any():
        tiers.append('persistent')
    if w['extreme'].any():
        tiers.append('extreme')
    if hit == len(w):
        n_caught += 1
        verdict = "CAUGHT"
    elif hit > 0:
        verdict = "PARTIAL"
    else:
        verdict = "MISSED"
    print(f"  {evt['article']:<16} x{evt['multiplier']:>4.0f} {evt['duration_days']}d   "
          f"{hit}/{len(w)} planted days flagged   "
          f"peak z={w['robust_z'].max():5.1f}   {verdict} ({' + '.join(tiers)})")

recall = n_caught / len(spike_events)
print(f"\nrecall on planted events: {n_caught}/{len(spike_events)} ({recall:.0%})")
print(f"planted days flagged:     {days_caught}/{days_planted}")

## the tier design earns its keep right here: the 1-day x12 spike has no
## persistence to offer, only the extreme tier saves it
musk = results[
    (results['article'] == 'Elon_Musk')
    & (results['timestamp'] == pd.Timestamp('2024-09-09'))
].iloc[0]
print(f"\nthe one-day Elon_Musk spike: persistent tier = {bool(musk['persistent'])}, "
      f"extreme tier = {bool(musk['extreme'])} (z={musk['robust_z']:.0f})")
print("a persistence-only detector throws this event away entirely")
```

One honest note on precision: I don't report a false positive rate against the planted
events, because the other flags aren't false. This window of real data contains the
Olympics, a rally shooting, a party convention, and the home stretch of an election.
Lots of pages genuinely spiked. The planted events answer "does the detector catch a
spike of known size," and the ranked event list answers "is the output triageable."

## The detector at work, on all four planted spikes

```{python}
#| label: chart-style
#| code-summary: "Shared chart style and helpers"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

## same palette as the rest of the portfolio. blue for data, slate for
## baselines, red strictly means detected / planted
NAVY, BLUE, SLATE, RED, GRAY = '#0f172a', '#2563eb', '#94a3b8', '#dc2626', '#64748b'

plt.rcParams.update({
    'axes.spines.top': False,
    'axes.spines.right': False,
    'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'font.size': 10,
})

# def headline(ax, title, sub):
#     ### takeaway big and bold, literal description small and gray
#     ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=30, color=NAVY)
#     ax.text(0, 1.06, sub, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY)
def headline(ax, title, subtitle):
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=30, color=NAVY)
    ax.text(0.0, 1.02, subtitle.strip(),
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY, ha='left', va='bottom')

```

Each panel below is one planted spike. The shaded band is the planted window (the answer
key), the dots are what the detector flagged. The grade is visual: dots inside every band.

```{python}
#| label: spike-viz
#| code-summary: "Planted windows vs detected days, one panel per event"
#| fig-cap: "The four planted spikes. Blue line: daily views (real data plus the planted multiplier). Gray band: the page's normal range, rolling median ±3 robust σ, anything outside it is an outlier candidate. Red band: the planted window. Red dots: days the detector flagged."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 10

# fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(13, 8.5))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6.5, 13))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, evt in zip(axes, spike_events):
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    art = results[results['article'] == evt['article']]

    ## the planted window first so everything draws on top of it
    ax.axvspan(s, e, color=RED, alpha=0.08, zorder=0)

    ## the normal range band. days outside this gray zone are what the
    ## detector considers outlier territory
    ax.fill_between(
        art['timestamp'],
        art['rolling_median'] - 3 * art['rolling_sigma'],
        art['rolling_median'] + 3 * art['rolling_sigma'],
        color=SLATE, alpha=0.18, zorder=1,
    )

    ax.plot(art['timestamp'], art['views'], color=BLUE, linewidth=1.1, zorder=2)
    ax.plot(art['timestamp'], art['rolling_median'], color=SLATE, linewidth=1.4, zorder=2)

    hits = art[art['is_spike']]
    ax.scatter(hits['timestamp'], hits['views'], color=RED, s=26, zorder=5)

    ## one annotation per panel: what we planted, anchored to the peak
    ## inside the planted window (the page max can be a real event, taylor's
    ## september endorsement spike actually tops her planted one)
    win = art[(art['timestamp'] >= s) & (art['timestamp'] < e)]
    peak = win.loc[win['views'].idxmax()]
    ax.annotate(
        f"planted x{evt['multiplier']:.0f}, {evt['duration_days']}d",
        xy=(peak['timestamp'], peak['views']),
        xytext=(12, -4), textcoords='offset points',
        fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold', color=RED,
    )

    ax.set_title(evt['article'].replace('_', ' '), fontsize=11.5,
                 fontweight='bold', loc='left', color=NAVY)
    ax.set_ylabel('daily views', fontsize=9)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
    ax.margins(y=0.1)

plt.suptitle('All four planted spikes caught, dots land inside every red window',
             fontsize=12.5, fontweight='bold', x=0.005, ha='left', color=NAVY)
# plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0, 1])
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96], h_pad=2.0)

plt.show()
```

## Validating the spikes I didn't plant

The Taylor Swift and Elon Musk panels show red dots *outside* the planted windows, so
either the detector is throwing false alarms on these pages or those are real events. We
can check, because every flag comes with a date and the news record for summer 2024 is
easy to audit.

```{python}
#| label: validate-natural
#| code-summary: "List every non-planted flagged day on the four planted pages"

print("flagged days on the planted pages, outside the planted windows:")
print("=" * 70)
for evt in spike_events:
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    extra = flagged[
        (flagged['article'] == evt['article'])
        & ~((flagged['timestamp'] >= s) & (flagged['timestamp'] < e))
    ]
    label = f"{len(extra)} day(s)" if len(extra) else "none, page was quiet"
    print(f"\n  {evt['article']} ({label}):")
    for _, r in extra.iterrows():
        print(f"    {r['timestamp'].date()}   {r['views']:>9,} views"
              f"   vs baseline {r['rolling_median']:>9,.0f}   z={r['robust_z']:.1f}")
```

Every one of those checks out against the news record, none of them are false alarms:

- **Taylor Swift, Aug 8** — her three Vienna Eras Tour shows were cancelled on Aug 7
  after authorities foiled a planned attack on the concerts. Huge global story.
- **Taylor Swift, Sep 11 to 13** — she endorsed Kamala Harris on Instagram the night of
  the Sep 10 presidential debate. Her page roughly quadrupled its baseline for two days.
- **Elon Musk, Jul 13 to 17** — the rally shooting happened Jul 13 and Musk endorsed
  Trump within hours, then spent the week in the news cycle (reports of a major pro-Trump
  PAC pledge followed). Four flagged days riding one news wave.

Einstein and Bitcoin show nothing outside their planted windows, which is its own kind of
validation: the detector stays quiet on pages where nothing happened. So on these four
pages the complete flag list is four planted spikes plus three real news stories, and
zero unexplained alarms.

## Why median + MAD instead of mean + std

This is the methodological core of the piece, so here's the failure made visible. Same
data (Taylor Swift with the planted ×3 spike), two detectors. The classic mean + 3·std
band swallows its own spike: the outlier days inflate the rolling std so much that the
threshold balloons above the data for the next three weeks. The robust band barely moves.

```{python}
#| label: robust-vs-classic
#| code-summary: "Classic mean+std threshold vs robust median+MAD on the same spike"
#| fig-cap: "Taylor Swift daily views with the planted ×3 spike. The classic threshold (dashed) inflates after contact with a spike and stays blind for a full window length, you can see it happen again after her real September spike. The robust threshold (solid navy) is barely disturbed."

ts = results[results['article'] == 'Taylor_Swift'].sort_values('timestamp').copy()

## classic detector on the identical window settings
ts['c_mean'] = ts['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).mean()
ts['c_std'] = ts['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).std()
ts['classic_upper'] = ts['c_mean'] + 3 * ts['c_std']
ts['robust_upper'] = ts['rolling_median'] + 3 * ts['rolling_sigma']

evt = [e for e in spike_events if e['article'] == 'Taylor_Swift'][0]
s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.axvspan(s, e, color=RED, alpha=0.08, zorder=0)
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['views'], color=BLUE, linewidth=1.2, label='daily views')
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['robust_upper'], color=NAVY, linewidth=2,label='robust threshold (median + 3·MADσ)')
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['classic_upper'], color=GRAY, linewidth=1.3,linestyle='--', label='classic threshold (mean + 3·std)')

## point at the failure, that's the whole chart
post = ts[(ts['timestamp'] > e) & (ts['timestamp'] <= e + pd.Timedelta(days=18))]
worst = post.loc[post['classic_upper'].idxmax()]
ax.annotate('the spike inflates the classic\nthreshold for 3 weeks after',
            xy=(worst['timestamp'], worst['classic_upper']),
            xytext=(25, -70), textcoords='offset points',
            fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold', color=GRAY,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=GRAY, lw=1.2,
                            connectionstyle='arc3,rad=0.25'))
ymin, ymax = ax.get_ylim()
ax.text(s, ymin + 0.04 * (ymax - ymin), f" planted ×{evt['multiplier']:.0f} window",
        fontsize=9, color=RED, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_ylabel('daily views')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', frameon=False)

headline(ax, 'Outliers raise a classic detector\'s own threshold',
         'same series, same window, two thresholds. robust statistics don\'t flinch')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## The real news comes along for free

The planted spikes grade the detector. But the more fun result is everything else it
found in the raw data. Plot every detected event by date and severity and the summer of
2024 writes its own timeline.

```{python}
#| label: events-timeline
#| code-summary: "Every detected event by peak date and severity"
#| fig-cap: "All detected events, peak robust z by peak date (log scale). Red: the four planted spikes. Blue: real events in the raw data. The Olympics band and the July 13 news cycle need no help from me."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

planted_set = {e['article'] for e in spike_events}
events['planted'] = events.apply(
    lambda r: any(
        e['article'] == r['article']
        and pd.Timestamp(e['start']) <= r['peak_date']
        < pd.Timestamp(e['start']) + pd.Timedelta(days=e['duration_days'])
        for e in spike_events
    ),
    axis=1,
)

import matplotlib.transforms as mtransforms

# fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))

nat = events[~events['planted']]
pla = events[events['planted']]
ax.scatter(nat['peak_date'], nat['peak_z'], s=34, color=BLUE, alpha=0.55, zorder=3)
ax.scatter(pla['peak_date'], pla['peak_z'], s=70, color=RED, zorder=4, marker='D')

## context bands: the olympics, and july 13. labels pinned just inside the
## top of the axes so they can't wander into the headline
trans = mtransforms.blended_transform_factory(ax.transData, ax.transAxes)
oly_s, oly_e = pd.Timestamp('2024-07-26'), pd.Timestamp('2024-08-11')
ax.axvspan(oly_s, oly_e, color='#f1f5f9', zorder=0)
ax.text(oly_s + (oly_e - oly_s) / 2, 0.96, 'Paris Olympics', ha='center',
        transform=trans, fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY, fontweight='bold')
j13 = pd.Timestamp('2024-07-13')
ax.axvline(j13, color=GRAY, linewidth=1, linestyle=':', zorder=1)
ax.text(j13, 0.88, 'Jul 13 rally shooting ', ha='right', transform=trans,
        fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY, fontweight='bold')

## label the planted four, custom offsets so einstein and gaga don't collide
planted_offsets = {
    'Albert_Einstein': (3, -17, 'left'),
    'Taylor_Swift': (8, 2, 'left'),
    'Bitcoin': (8, -3, 'left'),
    'Elon_Musk': (8, 2, 'left'),
}
for _, r in pla.iterrows():
    dx, dy, ha = planted_offsets.get(r['article'], (7, 4, 'left'))
    ax.annotate(r['article'].replace('_', ' '), xy=(r['peak_date'], r['peak_z']),
                xytext=(dx, dy), textcoords='offset points', ha=ha,
                fontsize=9, fontweight='bold', color=RED)

## plus the real events i can actually name
callouts = {
    'Lady_Gaga': ('sang at the opening ceremony', (8, -12)),
    'Theodore_Roosevelt': ('readers compare past assassination attempts', (-2, 12)),
    'Barack_Obama': ('DNC speech', (8, -1)),
}
for art, (why, (dx, dy)) in callouts.items():
    r = nat[nat['article'] == art].sort_values('peak_z', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    ax.annotate(f"{art.replace('_', ' ')}\n{why}", xy=(r['peak_date'], r['peak_z']),
                xytext=(dx, dy), textcoords='offset points',
                fontsize=8.5, color=NAVY, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_yscale('log')
ax.set_ylabel('peak robust z (log scale)')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.4)

headline(ax, 'The detector reconstructs the news cycle of summer 2024',
         f'\n{n_events} detected events across {n_flagged_articles} articles. red diamonds are the planted answer key, blue dots are real')

# plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The clusters are real news, found with zero knowledge of the news. Theodore Roosevelt,
JFK, Lincoln, and FDR all spike within a day of July 13 (readers looking up past
assassination attempts). The Eiffel Tower, Lady Gaga, and a dozen others light up when
the Olympics open on July 26. Obama spikes the morning after his August 20 DNC speech.
In a production media-monitoring setting this chart *is* the product: a ranked, dated
feed of "something happened to this page" with the sizes attached.

## Key Results

::: {.metrics-row}

::: {.metric}
[`{python} f"{n_caught}/{len(spike_events)}"`]{.metric-value}
[Planted fake spikes caught]{.metric-label}
[`{python} f"{days_caught}/{days_planted} planted days flagged"`]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[`{python} f"{compression_ratio:.1f}×"`]{.metric-value}
[Storage compression]{.metric-label}
[Parquet vs CSV, measured at render]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[`{python} f"{query_ms:.0f} ms"`]{.metric-value}
[Full warehouse scan]{.metric-label}
[Polars lazy query, measured at render]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[`{python} f"{n_events}"`]{.metric-value}
[Events ranked for triage]{.metric-label}
[Two-tier robust z detection]{.metric-detail}
:::

:::

## What this demonstrates

**The methodology:** spike detection with median + MAD instead of mean + std, because the
anomalies you're hunting will corrupt classical statistics (the comparison chart above
shows exactly how). Two detection tiers because one-day blips and slow burns are both
real and one rule can't serve both.

**The validation:** I planted four spikes with known sizes and dates and graded the
detector against them, instead of pointing at charts and declaring victory. The grading
is day-level, not "did the article show up somewhere in the flag list."

**The honesty:** the thresholds were tuned on this dataset and I say so. The planted
events measure recall, not precision, and I say that too. The real events the detector
surfaced (Olympics, July 13, the DNC) are the strongest evidence it works, precisely
because I didn't plant them.

## What I'd extend in a production version

- **CUSUM or Bayesian online changepoint detection** for slow level shifts, the MAD
  approach is built for sharp spikes
- **Per-series threshold learning** — one global z threshold across 103 very different
  pages is the biggest simplification here; volatility profiles differ by page
- **Cause attribution** — join detected events against news APIs, social trends, or a
  media schedule so events arrive pre-explained instead of pre-ranked

## Tools & Methods

::: {.skill-grid}
[Python]{.skill .primary}
[Polars]{.skill .primary}
[pyarrow]{.skill .primary}
[Parquet]{.skill .primary}
[pandas]{.skill}
[matplotlib]{.skill}
[Robust Statistics (MAD)]{.skill}
[Time-Series Analysis]{.skill}
[Anomaly Detection]{.skill}
[Hive Partitioning]{.skill}
:::
